agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Integration of two previously independent bounded contexts — Warehouse Management (stock) and Sales/Order Management (orders) — to enforce stock availability at order placement time and surface stock visibility to the sales team.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management. Currently these contexts are decoupled: orders are placed without checking stock. The feature request introduces a direct dependency from Sales to Warehouse.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Product","definition":"Exists in BOTH contexts. In Warehouse, it is a stockable item with a StockLevel. In Sales, it is a line-item reference (Product ID + Quantity). These are NOT the same concept — they are separate models that share an identity."},{"term":"StockLevel","definition":"Warehouse concept: the current quantity of a Product on hand. Integer >= 0. Owned exclusively by Context A."},{"term":"OrderLine","definition":"Sales concept: a reference to a Product ID and a requested Quantity within an Order."},{"term":"Block","definition":"AMBIGUOUS — not defined in either context today. Could mean: reject the order outright, place it in a held/pending state, or flag it for manual review."},{"term":"Stock Available Indicator","definition":"AMBIGUOUS — a new UI concept for the Sales team. Could mean a boolean (in stock / out of stock), a quantity, or a threshold-based signal (low/medium/high)."},{"term":"Real-time","definition":"AMBIGUOUS — could mean synchronous query at order-placement time, near-real-time via events (seconds of lag), or periodically refreshed cache."},{"term":"Insufficient Stock","definition":"AMBIGUOUS — could mean StockLevel < total requested Quantity for one order, or StockLevel < sum of all pending/unfulfilled orders' quantities (allocated stock)."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"StockLevel for any Product in Warehouse Management must never go below 0.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a physical constraint — you cannot ship items you do not have. Violating this corrupts warehouse state and leads to unfulfillable commitments.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: After any operation, Product.StockLevel >= 0 in the Warehouse context."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An Order may contain multiple OrderLines, each referencing a Product by ID and a Quantity.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a structural data integrity constraint defining the Order aggregate.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: Every OrderLine has a non-null Product ID and a Quantity > 0."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Stock availability checks must not grant the same stock unit to multiple orders (no overselling).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If two concurrent orders each see StockLevel=1 and both proceed, the warehouse invariant (BR-1) will be violated during fulfillment. The reservation/check mechanism must be concurrency-safe.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: The sum of all reserved/committed quantities for a Product never exceeds its StockLevel."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The Sales context must not directly mutate StockLevel in the Warehouse context.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Bounded context integrity — Warehouse owns its own data. Allowing Sales to write directly introduces tight coupling and bypasses warehouse business rules (e.g., auditing, lot tracking).","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: StockLevel changes only originate from Warehouse-owned operations (pick, receive, adjust)."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"The stock availability indicator shown to the Sales team may be eventually consistent with actual warehouse stock.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a UX/business policy decision. Perfect real-time consistency across bounded contexts is architecturally expensive and may not be required. A VP could decide acceptable staleness (e.g., 5 seconds vs 30 seconds).","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: The stock indicator reflects warehouse state within an agreed SLA (e.g., <= N seconds stale)."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer places an order with OrderLines totaling Quantity Q for Product P","when":"StockLevel of Product P in Warehouse is less than Q","then":"The order is blocked (exact blocking behavior TBD per ambiguity resolution — reject, hold, or flag)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A customer places an order with OrderLines totaling Quantity Q for Product P","when":"StockLevel of Product P in Warehouse is greater than or equal to Q","then":"The order is accepted and stock is reserved/committed so it cannot be double-sold"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Two customers simultaneously place orders that each require Quantity Q, and StockLevel = Q (only enough for one)","when":"Both orders attempt to reserve stock concurrently","then":"Exactly one order succeeds and one is blocked; StockLevel never goes below 0"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A sales team member is viewing the product catalog or order entry screen","when":"Stock levels change in the Warehouse context","then":"The stock available indicator updates within the agreed SLA without requiring a page refresh"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"The Warehouse system is temporarily unavailable","when":"A customer attempts to place an order","then":"The system degrades gracefully — either queues the order for later validation or rejects with a clear message (policy decision TBD)"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"An order with multiple OrderLines references Products P1, P2, P3","when":"P1 and P2 have sufficient stock but P3 does not","then":"The blocking behavior applies consistently — either the entire order is blocked (atomic) or only the insufficient line is flagged (partial). Policy TBD."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Concurrent orders exhaust stock simultaneously","expected_behavior":"The reservation mechanism must be atomic — only one order gets the last unit. Race conditions must not allow overselling."},{"scenario":"Stock replenished between check and order confirmation","expected_behavior":"If stock increases after a block decision, the order should be re-evaluable (if held) or the customer should be able to retry (if rejected)."},{"scenario":"Warehouse system unavailable during order placement","expected_behavior":"Degraded mode policy must be defined — fail-open (accept order, risk oversell) vs fail-closed (reject order, lose sale)."},{"scenario":"Order modification after stock reservation","expected_behavior":"If a customer increases quantity on an accepted order, the additional quantity must be re-checked against available (unreserved) stock."},{"scenario":"Order cancellation releasing reserved stock","expected_behavior":"Cancelled orders must release their stock reservation back to available inventory promptly."},{"scenario":"Stock indicator shows 'available' but order is blocked","expected_behavior":"Due to eventual consistency, the indicator may lag behind actual stock. The UI must communicate that availability is approximate and the order-time check is authoritative."},{"scenario":"Product exists in Sales but not yet in Warehouse","expected_behavior":"New products added to Sales catalog before warehouse setup must be handled — block by default or treat as zero stock."},{"scenario":"Multi-line order where stock is only partially available","expected_behavior":"Business must decide: all-or-nothing (atomic order) vs partial fulfillment with split shipments."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Reduces overselling losses and customer compensation costs. However, overly aggressive blocking may reject orders that could have been fulfilled (e.g., incoming shipment arriving same day), resulting in lost revenue. The stock indicator may increase sales conversion by giving the sales team confidence.","operational":"Introduces a runtime dependency between Sales and Warehouse systems. Warehouse downtime now directly impacts order placement — this is a significant operational coupling that did not exist before. Requires new infrastructure for cross-context communication (events, API, or shared read model).","user":"Sales team gains visibility into stock, improving their ability to set customer expectations. Customers benefit from not placing orders that can't be fulfilled. However, false negatives (blocking when stock is actually available due to stale data) will frustrate both sales and customers.","risk":"Primary risk is introducing tight coupling between two previously independent contexts. Secondary risk is the consistency model — eventual consistency means the indicator and the blocking check may disagree, confusing users. Third risk: without a proper reservation/allocation model, concurrent access to stock will cause either overselling or deadlocks.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Sales Team","impact":"Gains stock visibility (positive), but may face order rejections they cannot override (negative if no override mechanism exists). Workflow changes required."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Fewer unfulfillable orders (positive), but may be blocked from ordering when stock is actually available due to lag (negative)."},{"group":"Warehouse Team","impact":"Their system now has an external consumer with real-time expectations. Warehouse data quality and system uptime become critical-path for Sales."},{"group":"Engineering/Platform","impact":"Must build and maintain a cross-context integration layer — event bus, read model, or API gateway. Ongoing operational burden."},{"group":"Finance/Accounting","impact":"Fewer write-offs from overselling, but need to account for reserved-but-not-yet-shipped stock as a new inventory state."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The feature request is a single sentence with three ambiguous terms ('block', 'real-time', 'insufficient'). It introduces a cross-context integration without specifying the integration pattern, consistency requirements, failure mode, or exact blocking semantics. Multiple critical business decisions are unresolved.","high_confidence_areas":["StockLevel >= 0 is a hard invariant that must be preserved","Sales must not directly mutate Warehouse state — context boundaries must be respected","Concurrent stock access requires an atomic reservation mechanism to prevent overselling","This integration introduces operational coupling that did not previously exist"],"low_confidence_areas":["What 'block' means — reject, hold, or flag for review","Whether blocking is all-or-nothing per order or per order line","What 'real-time' means — synchronous check, event-driven, or polling","Failure mode when Warehouse is unavailable — fail-open vs fail-closed","Whether the stock indicator should show exact quantities or just a boolean/threshold","Whether reserved stock (committed to accepted-but-unshipped orders) should be subtracted from the displayed availability"],"assumptions":["The two contexts will communicate via an integration pattern (events or API) rather than sharing a database directly","Stock reservation is a new concept that must be introduced to bridge the gap between 'stock checked' and 'stock picked'","The stock indicator is a read model projected from Warehouse events, not a live query into the Warehouse database"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The term 'block' is undefined. It could mean: (A) reject the order outright with an error, (B) place the order in a 'held' or 'pending' state awaiting stock, or (C) flag the order for manual review by the sales team. Each option has vastly different domain model, UX, and workflow implications.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Customers","Warehouse Team"],"options":["Reject: order fails immediately, customer must retry later","Hold: order enters a pending state, automatically released when stock arrives","Flag: order is accepted but flagged, sales team manually decides"],"recommendation":"Recommend 'Hold' with automatic release — it preserves customer intent, avoids lost sales, and gives the warehouse time to replenish. But this requires a new Order state (Held/PendingStock) and an event-driven release mechanism."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Real-time' stock availability is undefined. Across bounded contexts, true real-time is architecturally expensive and may be unnecessary. The acceptable staleness window determines the entire integration architecture (synchronous API vs event-driven projection vs polling).","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Engineering/Platform"],"options":["Synchronous API call to Warehouse on every page load (true real-time, tight coupling)","Event-driven read model updated within seconds (near-real-time, loose coupling)","Periodic cache refresh every N minutes (simple, but stale)"],"recommendation":"Recommend event-driven read model with < 5 second propagation SLA. This balances freshness with architectural decoupling. The order-placement check should be a synchronous reservation call for correctness, while the UI indicator can be eventually consistent."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Insufficient stock' does not account for stock already reserved by other accepted-but-unshipped orders. Without an allocation/reservation model, the displayed StockLevel is misleading — it shows physical stock, not available-to-promise stock.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Customers","Finance/Accounting"],"options":["Check against physical StockLevel (simple, but allows overselling when multiple orders are in flight)","Introduce Available-To-Promise (ATP) = StockLevel minus reserved quantities (correct, but requires new domain concept)"],"recommendation":"Introduce Available-To-Promise (ATP) as a first-class domain concept in the Warehouse context. This is the only way to prevent overselling without serializing all order placement."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Multi-line order behavior when only some products have sufficient stock is unspecified. Should the entire order be blocked atomically, or should individual lines be flaggable while the rest proceed?","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Customers"],"options":["Atomic: entire order blocked if any line has insufficient stock","Partial: block only insufficient lines, accept the rest (split fulfillment)","Customer choice: present the shortage and let the customer decide"],"recommendation":"Recommend atomic blocking by default with an option for the sales team to override and accept partial fulfillment. This prevents unexpected split shipments while giving the sales team flexibility."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

Currently these contexts are decoupled: orders are placed without checking stock. The feature request introduces a direct depen

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this domain integration scenario carefully.

This is about integrating two bounded contexts: Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management. The feature request wants to couple them by checking stock during order placement and showing real-time stock availability.

Key domain concerns:
1. Cross-context coupling - Sales currently doesn't check Warehouse
2. "Real-time" stock indicator - eventual consistency issues
3. "Automatically block" - who owns the blocking decision?
4. The term "Product" appears in both contexts - shared kernel or separate models?

This has clear ambiguities: "real-time" is vague, "block" is vague (reject? hold? queue?), and the integration pattern isn't specified.


The requirements use imprecise language that could mean different things depending on the implementation approach - "automatically block if stock insufficient" and "real-time stock available indicator" both need clarification before I can recommend a specific integration pattern.
